texts.label: text
texts.orig: Human respiration produces carbon dioxide (CO2), which as the concentration rises in the relatively small volume of a spaceship or submarine, would result in performance impairment and unconsciousness (Ge et al., 2002). Therefore, control of CO2 within a certain range is one of the most important tasks in closed life support systems (Zhang et al., 2007). Importantly, CO2 removal technologies in life support systems must be safe and reliable, which is characterized by small volume, low mass, low rate of energy use, minimal use of consumables, and simple and convenient operation (Cheng et al., 2008). For applications at low concentration CO2 ( o 1%, v/v) and near ambient temperature, carbonic anhydrase (CA) is the most efficient catalyst for CO2 hydration and dehydration, with a turnover number of 10 6 mol CO2mol � 1 CAs � 1 (Bao and Trachtenberg, 2006). The reaction rate catalyzed by CA is much faster than the rate at which CO2 complexes with chemical absorber molecules such as amines (Alper et al., 1980), including monoethanolamine (MEA) (Teramoto et al., 1996), diethanolamine (DEA) (Davis and Sandall, 1993), and ethylenediamine (EDA) (Matsuyama et al., 1994).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper reported the results of developing a novel hollow fiber membrane reactor contained immobilized enzyme for selective separation of low concentration CO2 from mixed gas streams. In the reactor, two bundles of poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) hollow fiber membranes were aligned staggered parallel in a tube module, and lab-made poly(acrylic acidco -acrylamide)/hydrotalcite (PAAAAm/HT) nanocomposite hydrogel was filled between fibers, in which carbonic anhydrase (CA enzyme) was immobilized. The effects of CA concentration, buffer concentration, the flow rate of sweep gas, operational temperature, and CO2 concentration on separation performance were investigated in detail. The results showed that the transport resistance was mainly from the hydrogel layer, and decreased greatly with immobilization of carbonic anhydrase in hydrogel. Moreover, immobilized CA could retain over 76% enzymatic activity and thermal stability was also improved. The data showed that this enzyme-based membrane reactor could effectively separate CO2 at low concentration from mixed gas streams. For the feed with 0.1% (v/v) of CO2, the selectivity of CO2/N2 was 820, CO2/O2 was 330, and CO2 permeance was 1.65 -10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa. Prolonged runs lasting 30h showed that separation performances of the membrane reactor were quite stable.

Prolonged runs lasting 30h showed that separation performances of the membrane reactor were quite stable.

texts.orig: CA catalyzes the reversible hydration of CO2 by a two-step process (see Fig. 1). In the first step, CA hydrolyzes H2O to capture a hydroxyl releasing a proton. In the second step, the hydroxyl attacks the carbonyl bond of CO2 to yield bicarbonate and the unreacted enzyme. The hydration step occurs at the CO2 rich side. At the CO2 lean side the reverse reactions occur, liberating CO2 (Cowan et al., 2003; Duda et al., 2001; Elder et al., 2004; Pocker and Bjorkquist, 1977).

texts.orig: On the other hand, compared with other approaches, liquid membrane technologies are attractive in CO2 capture or separation, typically for low or moderate concentration CO2 because they decrease transport resistance so that high permeance is achieved together with high selectivity (Cowan et al., 2003). Using CA as a catalyst, the design of an immobilized liquid membrane (ILM) or a supported liquid membrane (SLM) has shown the potential to remove low concentration CO2. Enns (1967) showed that CA, in the presence of a phosphate buffer, could increase CO2 transfer across a SLM (150 m m thick) by as much as 500-fold depending on pH and CA concentration. However, in the subsequent two decades, none of these succeeded in reporting a stable, high efficiency design. One of the important reasons was the instability of SLM or ILM due to evaporative loss and subsequent deactivation of the chemical facilitator (Kemperman et al., 1996). Later SLM or ILM evolved into contained liquid membranes (CLM) (Guha et al., 1992). In CLM operation, a thin film of water or aqueous solution is held in the interstitial space between two independent sets of intimately commingled hydrophobic microporous hollow fibers. One set of fibers carries a feed gas, and the other set carries a sweep gas. The stability of CLM design has been enhanced greatly by holding the aqueous solution within the interstitial space of hydrophobic surfaces. Cowan et al. (2003) provided a first level optimization of CA-based contained liquid membrane (CLM) system and proved its utility as a means for separating CO2 from low concentration CO2 sources, such as air and respiratory gas. Given a feed concentration of 0.1% (v/v) CO2, the selectivity of CO2/N2 was 1090, CO2/O2 was 790, and CO2 permeance was 4.71 -10 � 8 mol/m 2 s Pa. The CLM design results in a system that was very stable even in the presence of dry feed and sweep gas. However, the short catalytic lifetime of free enzyme limits their usefulness and the loss of enzyme solution could not be solved completely.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Immobilization of enzyme has been taken to improve catalytic stability of enzymes and can expand the applications of the natural catalysts (Kim and Grate, 2003; Wang and Hsieh, 2008). Coating CA with a Michael-adduct-based membrane could retain only 7% of the original activity (Drevon et al., 2003). Immobilization of CA on methacrylic acid polymers is not suitable for the hydration of CO2 that occurs at the gas-liquid interface due to the extra mass transport resistance caused by the presence of the porous matrix (Azari and Nemat-Gorgani, 1999). There has been an increasing interest on the synthesis of hydrogels for enzyme immobilization. Several researchers reported that hydrogel carriers such as poly (acrylamideco -acrylic acid), and Ca-alginate provided a protective free-water microenvironment for enzymes and yield higher stabilities and activity (Yahs -i et al., 2005; S -ahin et al., 2005). Cheng et al. (2008) reported a hollow fiber contained hydrogel-CA membrane contactor for CO2 removal from the enclosed spaces. A poly (acrylic acidco -acrylamide) hydrogel was prepared for the immobilization of CA. Activity of immobilized CA could be maintained without apparent reduction during the investigation period of 6 months. By combination of both advantages of CO2 facilitated transport using CA and the hydrogel, the hydrogel-CA CLM showed good CO2 removal capability, reducing CO2 from 0.52% in the inlet feed gas to below 0.090%. These hydrogels have good water-retention capacity and the loss of membrane liquid could be mitigated. However, in order to compress the volume of the CA-based CLM, CO2 removal capability should be further improved. This problem could be solved by improving salt-absorbency of hydrogels. Hydrogels with high salt-absorbency could immobilize more CA solution to enhance the performance of the CA-based CLM. Therefore, we have prepared a novel poly (acrylic acidco -acrylamide)/ hydrotalcite (PAA-AAm/HT) nanocomposite hydrogel for the CA immobilization (Zhang et al., 2009a, b). Water and saltabsorbency of the nanocomposite hydrogel was improved greatly in comparison with common hydrogels. Moreover, immobilized CA maintains over 76% enzymatic activity due to the formation of a microenvironment almost all composed of free water inside the porous network structures of the nanocomposite hydrogel.

Cheng et al. (2008) reported a hollow fiber contained hydrogel-CA membrane contactor for CO2 removal from the enclosed spaces. A poly (acrylic acidco -acrylamide) hydrogel was prepared for the immobilization of CA. Activity of immobilized CA could be maintained without apparent reduction during the investigation period of 6 months. By combination of both advantages of CO2 facilitated transport using CA and the hydrogel, the hydrogel-CA CLM showed good CO2 removal capability, reducing CO2 from 0.52% in the inlet feed gas to below 0.090%. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, a thin PAA-AAm/HT nanocomposite hydrogel film with immobilized CA was held in the interstitial space between two independent sets of intimately commingled hydrophobic microporous PVDF hollow fibers. A novel hollow fiber contained immobilized-enzyme membrane reactor was prepared for the selective separation of low concentration CO2. The effects of CA concentration, buffer concentration, and pH, the flow rate of sweep gas, operational temperature and CO2 concentration on the performance were investigated in detail.
texts.text: In this study, a thin PAA-AAm/HT nanocomposite hydrogel film with immobilized CA was held in the interstitial space between two independent sets of intimately commingled hydrophobic microporous PVDF hollow fibers. A novel hollow fiber contained immobilized-enzyme membrane reactor was prepared for the selective separation of low concentration CO2. The effects of CA concentration, buffer concentration, and pH, the flow rate of sweep gas, operational temperature and CO2 concentration on the performance were investigated in detail.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 shows that the membrane reactor is constructed by two independent sets of intimately commingled hydrophobic microporous PVDF hollow fibers containing immobilized enzyme with nanocomposite hydrogels. Two nylon meshes were used as the spacer to control the thickness of hydrogel layer and the thickness was about 1.5mm. The geometrical characteristics of the membrane reactor are listed in Table 1. PVDF hollow fibers used in this experiment are prepared in our lab, hydrophobic microporous, 60% porosity, with 220nm -380nm pores and a tortuosity value of 1.5. The outer and inner layer morphology of PVDF hollow fiber is showed in Fig. 3. The fabrication of the membrane module involved preparation of a fiber bundle and putting them in a shell and finally potting the ends with a resin mixture to form a tube sheet. Two sets of fibers of known length and numbers were placed close to each other, rolled together in the middle up to a particular length while the two ends were kept separate. One set of fibers (27 fibers) carried the feed gas and the other (26 fibers) the sweep gas.

texts.orig: The schematic diagram of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 4. The feed gas stream consists of a mixture of CO2 and air. The desired CO2 concentration was obtained by an air compressor (Hangzhou Hang Kong Compressor Co., Ltd.). Delivery of all gases was regulated by mass flow controllers. The feed side pressure was controlled at 5 -10 5 Pa by a back-pressure regulator (Tescom, USA). Helium (He) was supplied to the permeate side as a sweep gas and the pressure was kept constant at almost atmospheric pressure. Compositions of all gas streams were analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC) (Agilent Model 1790, Agilent Technologies)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas separation usually uses an inert gas such as argon or helium as the sweep gas to remove permeates from the sweep side. To achieve high efficiency for the membrane reactor, it is pivotal to control the flow rate of feed and sweep gas. The appropriate flow rate of feed and sweep gases could enhance the specific surface area of the gas-liquid contact to improve the removal efficiency. Moreover, CO2 could be hydrated to bicarbonate as soon as possible. We found that CO2 transport could be facilitated if the flow rate in the feed side ( VF ) was lower than that in the sweep side ( VS ). However, if VS increased continuatively, the separation performance of the reactor would reduce. If the flow rate of sweep gas was too large, the free water on the surface of the hydrogel layer would be taken away partly resulting in the formation of dense skin layer on the surface of the hydrogel. Therefore, the mass transfer resistance of the membrane reactor would increase gradually resulting in the decrease of the separation performance of the membrane reactor. In order to prove the loss of the free water on the surface of the hydrogel layer, the blue silica gel was used in the outlet of the sweep gas (see Fig. 4). In the course of the experiment, as VS increased, the color of the blue silica gel changed gradually, until the pink. The result showed that too large flow rate of sweep gas could take away the free water on the surface of the hydrogel layer partly. Consequently, a moderate flow rate of sweep gas is good selection to produce high driving force.

texts.orig: The effect of buffer concentration on the separation performance of the membrane reactor is shown in Fig. 6. Four concentrations of Tris-HCl buffer were used for the experiments, 10, 20, 30, and 40mM. The selectivity increased as the buffer concentration increased from 10 to 30mM. Then, there was a slight decrease at 40mM. Compared with the selectivity, CO2 permeance changed little. The result shows that the selectivity for CO2/N2 and CO2/O2 reached maxima at a buffer concentration of 30mM. The best Tris-HCl concentration is estimated in the range of 20-30mM. Moreover, the separation performance at different pH values was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 7. Similarly, there was an optimum pH value (pH 8) for good performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here k is the measured rate constant, k 0 is the zero ionic strength rates constant, ZA and ZB are the electrostatic charges of the reacting species, and I represents the ionic strength of the solution. If the charges are opposite then there is a decrease in the reaction rate with increasing ionic strength whereas if the charges are identical, an increase in the reaction rate will occur. Therefore, as the Tris-HCl buffer concentration increased, the reaction rate increased. However, if the concentration is too high, it will affect the enzyme activity and the bulk pH value and the reactor rate will decrease. Indeed, enzyme conformations, stability, and activity are a result of a complex interplay of factors. These are usually involved in separate competing terms, such as, electrostatic, dipolar, and Van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonds, polarization effects, and association-dissociation equilibrium of charged groups (Salis et al., 2007). The ionic strength of solutions is as an important issue as pH. In addition, salting-out effect caused by buffer solution could decrease solubility of nonreactive gases, N2 or O2, and thereby increased the selectivity of CO2/N2 and CO2/O2. However, there is a trade-off between permeance and selectivity, and an optimized buffer concentration is needed.

texts.orig: Since the solubility of bicarbonate (with Na + as a counter ion, bicarbonate solubility is 9.32 wt%, 2.1 M at 25 1 C) is much more than that of CO2 in water (0.053 wt%, 0.012 M at 25 1 C). So the transport capacity for CO2 is much higher in bicarbonate form than for CO2( aq ) even though the diffusivity for CO2( aq ) is greater than that for bicarbonate (Cowan et al., 2003). That is, bicarbonate transport greatly improved the total CO2 mass transfer. Therefore, when the CA concentration was low, there was less CA enzyme to hydrate and dehydrate all the CO2 at a maximum rate. Thus CO2 permeance increased with the increasing the amount of CA enzyme. Because O2 and N2 cannot react with CA enzyme, the selectivity increased as the CA concentration increased.

texts.orig: In order to study the utilization of enzyme, we calculated and obtained the actual utilization rate of enzyme. For CA concentration of 1000 mg/L, the actual amount of CA is 6.67 -10 � 6 mol. If all the enzymes are involved in catalytic reaction, the ideal amount of CO2 that can be hydrated is 6.67 mol CO2/s. Beside the CA concentration, the CA distribution in the hydrogel layers might also limit the role of CA for the hydration and dehydration. The fluorescence photo of the hydrogel with immobilized enzyme is shown in Fig. 9 and the bright dots were labeled CA with FITC. The result showed that CA enzymes were dispersed uniformly in the nanocomposite hydrogel.

texts.orig: For the application of the technology in CO2 separation for the closed spaces, CO2 concentration should be controlled to values lower than 0.5% at 101.325kPa (Cheng et al., 2008). It is important to investigate the effect of different CO2 concentration on the separation performance of the membrane reactor. The permeance and selectivity as a function of the CO2 concentration in the feed is shown in Fig. 11. Both CO2 permeance and selectivity of CO2/N2, CO2/O2 decrease as CO2 concentration in the feed side is increased. texts.orig: Enzyme activity is typically a linear function of temperature. The upper temperature bound of CA enzyme is about 40 1 C (Cowan et al., 2003). In this study, the thermal stability of the free CA and the immobilized CA were determined at 20 and 50 1 C, respectively. As shown in Fig. 13, the immobilized CA on the hydrogels exhibited significantly improved stability at 50 1 C, at which the free CA lost almost all of its activity in 15min, while the activity of the immobilized CA decreased to 65% of its original activity after 60min. Moreover, immobilized CA enzyme retained about 94% of its original activity after 60min at 20 1 C. This enhanced stability was attributed to the CA molecules embedding inside the porous network structure of the nanocomposite hydrogel that strengthened the secondary structure stability of CA enzyme. The effect of operational temperature on the separation performance was investigated. Fig. 14 illustrates the effect of temperature on separation performance. Although temperature could impact the enzymatic activity resulting in the slight decrease of enzymatic activity, the solubility and diffusion of CO2 in the hydrogel layer were improved at higher operating temperature. Therefore, both CO2 permeance and selectivity of CO2/N2, CO2/O2 have a slight decrease as temperature is increased. The maximum operational temperature could be achieved at 60 1 C. Beyond 60 1 C, the separation performance dropped a little. The possible reason is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 15 illustrates the operational stability of the membrane reactor. The result showed that CO2 removal capacity changed little in a long time of 30 h at 20 1 C. This is the major advantage of the nanocomposite hydrogel that a microenvironment was formed almost all composed of free water in the porous structure, which provided the nicer survival condition for the CA enzyme. Importantly, immobilized CA enzyme retained about 94% of its original activity after 60 min at 20 1 C (see Fig. 13). Moreover, the platelets of HT were exfoliated and dispersed in the polymer matrix, causing more nanoscale layers to form and providing a stronger hydration ability of the hydroxyl groups. Because the water trapped into the network of the porous nanocomposite hydrogel is not easily lost with the flow of both gases, the stability of the membrane reactor was improved compared with contained enzyme liquid membrane (Cheng et al., 2008).
